title: The Hike

jokes.text: Moira announces 'Dobro jutro, my darlings! That, of course, is good morning in...' and her children cut her off with 'Bosnian. We know!'
jokes.setup: Moira dramatically announces a foreign phrase as if her children have never heard it before
jokes.punchline: David and Alexis finish her sentence flatly — a well-worn routine they're exhausted by
jokes.explanation: Instantly establishes Moira's performative cosmopolitanism and the kids' weary familiarity with it. The simultaneous correction is a clean, efficient bit that shows the family dynamic without overstaying. Character-specific but not transcendent.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: As they say in French, 'c'est la vie'—which means—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We know what it means, Mother.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, we went to school.

jokes.text: David immediately asks 'Okay, have we done something? I'm so confused.' when Moira arrives with breakfast in bed
jokes.setup: Moira shows up with pastries and coffee, claiming she just wants to be near her children
jokes.punchline: David's immediate suspicious response — any act of generosity from Moira must have ulterior motives
jokes.explanation: David's paranoia is fully earned through seasons of Moira's self-interest. The line is perfectly in character and sets up the reveal that she does, in fact, want something. The comedy is in how right he is.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why are you in my room? What did you do? What's happening?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Can't a mother bring her son breakfast in bed?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No. No, she cannot. Not in this family. What do you want?

jokes.text: Moira attributes her visit to 'the heightened emotionality of opening night just around the corner, and... my bebe girl leaving me so soon!'
jokes.setup: She's been asked what she wants; she denies wanting anything
jokes.punchline: Her explanation is so overwrought and theatrical that it's transparently a performance rather than genuine emotion
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira deflection — she uses theatrical emotion to avoid accountability. Functional character comedy but not a standout joke in isolation; it mainly serves to build the scene.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I've prepared a petite patisserie breakfast for us this morning.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Are these the lobby pastries from the hotel?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, they've been unfolded in such a way that really allows their true potential to unfold.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You just put them on a plate.

jokes.text: David looks at the coffee Moira brought and says 'Okay well, this coffee has nothing in it.' Moira replies 'Oh, it's just a gesture, David! Stop being so literal.'
jokes.setup: Moira presents coffee as a gift to soften them up for the favor she needs
jokes.punchline: The coffee is empty — a gesture so hollow it's literally hollow — and she defends it as conceptually sufficient
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's first great joke. The empty coffee is both a perfect visual metaphor for Moira's parenting (all presentation, no substance) and a genuinely surprising, specific comedic detail. 'It's just a gesture, David! Stop being so literal.' is immediately quotable and captures Moira's philosophical approach to gifts. High originality — not a joke you could easily predict — and economically devastating.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I have a disability. I cannot fold.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You don't have a disability.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I do. It's from a lifetime of signing checks, not real work.

jokes.text: David asks 'Aren't these just the cinnamon buns from the lobby?' about the pastries Moira brought
jokes.setup: Moira has presented the pastries as if she found a charming 'petite patisserie'
jokes.punchline: They're just the lobby cinnamon buns — she didn't go anywhere special at all
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Moira's grandiosity — the 'charming patisserie' is just their own lobby. Clean setup/payoff, and it matters later when the programs/cinnamon bun detail connects back to John's heartburn. Not laugh-out-loud but a well-planted moment.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: There's nothing in this coffee.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, David, the coffee is really just a vehicle for the cup. The actual coffee is an irrelevant detail.

jokes.text: Moira describes the 500 programs as having 'presented themselves unfolded' — a passive construction that removes all agency from the fact that she simply didn't fold them
jokes.setup: She's building to asking her children to fold 500 programs
jokes.punchline: The programs 'presented themselves unfolded' — Moira's syntactic gymnastics to avoid saying 'I forgot to fold them'
jokes.explanation: Deeply Moira-specific — her verbal self-mythology extends even to inanimate objects that 'present themselves' to her. The passive voice construction is genuinely clever and could only come from this character. Strong character integration and originality.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, I have been to every beach in the world, so I think I'm pretty much an expert. I got my open water certification in Cabo, and I've summered in every major coastal destination. So yeah, I'm basically a beach professional.

jokes.text: Moira explains she can't fold programs herself because of her 'crippling carpal tunnel from all those years of signing cheques'
jokes.setup: Moira needs someone to fold 500 programs for her
jokes.punchline: Her excuse for not doing it herself is the wrist strain from signing too many cheques — a rich-person problem so specific it's absurd
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'signing cheques' as the cause of her wrist injury is what makes this land — it's not just 'I'm delicate,' it's a callback to a life of conspicuous wealth. The joke earns its absurdity through character logic.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, I'm not going to actually use any of this stuff, but I love watching other people enjoy things.

jokes.text: Alexis mentions that 'Turkish Cosmo once included my photo on their list of the world's best sarongs'
jokes.setup: Ted is trying to discuss practical Galapagos gear
jokes.punchline: Alexis pivots to her modeling credentials, which are simultaneously glamorous, specific, and completely inapplicable
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Turkish Cosmo' and 'world's best sarongs' is Alexis's brand of comedy — her former life of glamour intruding on practical reality. The non sequitur nature and her pride in this credential is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: We could get mosquito nets. You know, romantic ones?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Mosquito nets? Well, actually, those can be quite dangerous. If there's a fire, you could get tangled in the netting and be unable to escape, leading to severe burns or smoke inhalation. Plus, if you have a medical emergency during the night, the net could impede first responders from reaching you quickly. And there's always the risk of the net catching on something and restricting your airway.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: But sure, let's get some mosquito nets.

jokes.text: Alexis says 'I don't actually go in the open water, but I can't wait to see you do that' about snorkeling
jokes.setup: Ted is packing snorkels for their Galapagos trip
jokes.punchline: Alexis casually admits she won't participate but frames it as supportive enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Good Alexis moment — she's genuinely supportive in her own way, but the gap between what a normal girlfriend would say ('I can't wait to try it!') and her actual response is the joke. Economy is strong.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Excuse me? I'm 32. I don't need Botox.

jokes.text: Ted mentions malaria pills and insect repellant; Alexis suggests 'one of those sexy little mosquito nets for around our bed' and Ted explains they're 'an insane safety hazard — if they catch fire, you're basically trapped in a burning cage'
jokes.setup: Alexis tries to make their tropical disease prevention romantic
jokes.punchline: Ted's matter-of-fact safety explanation completely demolishes the romance, followed by his deflating 'But, totally.'
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: Alexis's oblivious glamorization of danger (sexy burning cage) and Ted's reflex to correct her with genuine concern before remembering he wants to be agreeable. 'But, totally.' is a small gem of relationship comedy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A picnic? At a rest stop? Patrick, rest stops aren't destinations, they're pit stops for people who couldn't hold it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The ambiance is fluorescent lighting and the faint aroma of industrial bathroom cleaner mixed with regret.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And those tables are bolted down because apparently someone once tried to steal a picnic table from a rest stop, and now we all suffer the consequences.

jokes.text: Ted suggests Alexis think of the vaccine like Botox. Alexis recoils: 'Ew, Ted! What am I, 32?'
jokes.setup: Ted tries to make vaccines palatable for needle-averse Alexis by comparing them to something she'd welcome
jokes.punchline: Alexis is offended not by the Botox comparison but by the implication that she's old enough to need Botox
jokes.explanation: A genuinely great joke. The misdirection is perfect: we expect Alexis to be offended by the needle comparison, but she's actually offended by the implication she's 32. The specificity of '32' (not 30, not 40) is brilliantly calibrated to Alexis's vanity. Short, perfectly structured, highly quotable, and could only be delivered by Alexis. One of the episode's best.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You're always saying you need more physical activity.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I only say that so you'll tell me I don't need to.

jokes.text: Sound effect: 'Oof!' as David presumably reacts to starting the hike / getting out of the car
jokes.setup: Scene transition to the hiking location
jokes.punchline: David's immediate audible suffering before the hike has even begun
jokes.explanation: Short and efficient — David's exertion-averse character expressed in a single syllable. Works as a transition beat but doesn't transcend its function.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I just want a romantic picnic. Is that so much to ask?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You're describing a scene from a movie. A rom-com, specifically. Where they have a blanket, and there's no bugs, and someone has somehow made pasta that stays hot in a wicker basket.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: So, that's not real life, David.

jokes.text: David: 'Yeah, I just sorta feel like picnicking by the side of the road is basically an invitation to be murdered.'
jokes.setup: Patrick has brought them to what David thinks is a roadside picnic spot
jokes.punchline: David's dramatic leap to murder as the logical outcome of a roadside picnic
jokes.explanation: Classic David catastrophizing — turning minor inconveniences into existential threats. The urban anxiety applied to an entirely safe rural setting is very him. Not a top-tier joke but reliably funny.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You've got that working-man glow about you, Johnny.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Thank you, Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's nice. You're sweating through your shirt like a slab of ribs on a summer barbecue.

jokes.text: Patrick reveals they're going on a hike. David protests: 'Yes, so that you can tell me I don't need to!' — about wanting to do more physical activity
jokes.setup: Patrick points out David always says he wants to do more physical activity
jokes.punchline: David clarifies — he says it expecting reassurance that he doesn't need to, not expecting to actually exercise
jokes.explanation: Excellent character joke. The insight that David expresses self-improvement desires in order to be talked out of them is deeply true to his personality and rings recognizable for the audience too. Good structure and high character integration. Quotable and relatably absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira's taken half the furniture from this room to use as 'set pieces' for her community theater production.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: They're not set pieces, Johnny. They're practical, functional elements that add texture and authenticity to the staging.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, you've turned the motel office into a backstage area. We have guests.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Which is exactly why the Rosebud Motel is the perfect venue. It has that authentic, dilapidated charm that says 'community theater.'

jokes.text: David describes his picnic vision: 'on a charming park bench in the middle of a private English garden.' Patrick: 'David, you have to stop watching Notting Hill. It's not helpful for our relationship.'
jokes.setup: David explains what he was expecting the picnic to be
jokes.punchline: Patrick's diagnosis that David's romantic expectations are shaped by a late-90s rom-com, and his framing it as a relationship issue
jokes.explanation: Charming joke about David's idealized, media-shaped romantic expectations. 'It's not helpful for our relationship' is a perfectly deadpan way of framing Hugh Grant movies as a couples issue. Good economy and character specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, at your age, a lot of things slip through your fingers. Especially your fingers.

jokes.text: Moira describes John as her 'handsome proletarian. All rumpled and sweaty.' then adds 'I could do without the musky aroma though, whew!'
jokes.setup: John has been doing manual labor loading furniture onto Roland's truck
jokes.punchline: Moira romanticizes his working-class appearance before undercutting it with aristocratic distaste for his smell
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira contradiction — she loves the idea of her husband doing working-class labor in the abstract but can't tolerate the reality of it. 'Proletarian' as an endearment is perfectly her. The pivot to 'musky aroma, whew!' is well-timed.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Your father is like a bashful clam. He just clams up and won't communicate.

jokes.text: John: 'I didn't think you'd be robbing us blind.' Moira: 'According to Mrs. Rose, our motel is the only place sad enough to pass for a pre-war Berlin brothel.'
jokes.setup: John complains that Moira has taken too much furniture from the motel for her Cabaret set
jokes.punchline: Moira cheerfully reports her own assessment that their home/business qualifies as a pre-war brothel — and is proud of this
jokes.explanation: Sharp and dark. The joke works because Moira is both the victim of the insult (it's her motel) and the author of it — and she delivers it with zero irony. 'Pre-war Berlin brothel' is a specific, evocative, and genuinely funny image. Strong character integration since only Moira would say this about her own home.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Can we call your cousin? He had the same symptoms.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, he did, yeah. He died.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Just like your youth, right, old man?' after John drops the furniture
jokes.setup: John has just hurt himself dropping a piece of furniture
jokes.punchline: Roland's immediately callous age joke, which is darkly ironic given John is about to have a medical emergency
jokes.explanation: Weak as a standalone joke — it's a generic old-man jab — but gains rewatch value because it immediately precedes John's chest pain. The dramatic irony makes it land differently on second viewing. Roland's obtuseness is consistent with his character.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Oh my God! A heart attack? At what age?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Because I have been having palpitations. Chronic palpitations. I've been meaning to see someone about it, but I've just been so busy with my vocal exercises and my skincare routine.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Do you think it's hereditary? Can you catch a heart attack?

jokes.text: John tells Moira he felt 'a little tightening this morning when I picked up that box of programs from the printer's' — the programs Moira asked him to carry
jokes.setup: John is having a potential cardiac episode; Moira asks how long he's been in pain
jokes.punchline: The symptoms started because of the programs — the same programs Moira wanted everyone to fold — connecting her selfishness to his medical emergency
jokes.explanation: The callback to the programs is structurally satisfying — the object of Moira's trivial obsession becomes the cause of John's medical crisis. Dark but earned. The joke is mostly structural; the line itself isn't quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: He taught me tarot, and one of the first readings he did for me, he predicted that someone very close to me would soon be leaving my life forever.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Turns out he was just talking about himself.

jokes.text: Moira: 'You keep everything inside, like a bashful clam!'
jokes.setup: Moira is confronting John about not telling her he was in pain
jokes.punchline: 'A bashful clam' — a Moira-ism that mixes genuine tenderness with absurd metaphor
jokes.explanation: A genuine standout Moira-ism. 'Bashful clam' is original, absurd, and somehow perfectly captures both clam (closed-off) and bashful (shy). The fact that she delivers it with complete earnestness in a moment of genuine concern is peak Moira. Highly quotable and feels like it could only come from this character and this actress. One of the episode's best lines.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, so are we raising money for charity or for a new dishwasher?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Both, darling. Multi-purpose fundraising.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not how fundraising works. People need to know what they're donating towards.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David's right. We can't just tell people the money goes to charity and then buy a dishwasher.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Thank you. It's called integrity. Something this family could use more of.

jokes.text: 'That sounds alarmist. Right, John?'
jokes.setup: Stevie suggests they take John to the hospital because he's clearly unwell
jokes.punchline: Moira's instinct is to dismiss the medical emergency as overreaction — and she turns to the sick man himself for validation
jokes.explanation: Sharply funny. Moira's self-protective denial — and the absurd logic of consulting the potentially dying person about whether he needs medical care — is very character-specific. The brevity makes it land hard.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: I'm sensing something about your trip... Oh, I see a vision forming. You're going to have a terrible time.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Actually, it's getting more specific. You're going to miss your flight, and then your luggage will be lost.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Wait, it's getting worse. You'll be stuck in the airport for 14 hours, and the only food available will be a sad sandwich from 2003.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Oh my God, now I'm seeing... you'll sit next to someone who clips their toenails. The entire flight. Both feet.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: And when you finally arrive at your destination, your hotel room will overlook a construction site. There will be jackhammering at 5 AM.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: You'll get food poisoning from the minibar nuts, and then... oh, it's so vivid now... you'll slip in the shower and pull down the curtain rod, creating a moment of such profound humiliation that a single tear will roll down your cheek as a spotlight slowly fades to black while melancholic piano music plays.

jokes.text: Roland mentions his cousin Lenny had the same thing as John. Moira: 'Well, what did your cousin do? Can we call him?' Roland: 'You can try. I don't think you'll have much luck. The poor guy died of a heart attack.'
jokes.setup: Roland is trying to comfort Moira with the story of his cousin's similar condition
jokes.punchline: The cousin died — so the 'comforting' anecdote is the opposite of comforting
jokes.explanation: Well-constructed dark joke. The setup ('can we call him?') sets up a perfectly reasonable expectation, and Roland's delivery of the punchline with total obliviousness is character-true. The beat of 'You can try, I don't think you'll have much luck' elongates the suspense before the reveal perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, is this for a party?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: No, it's for a funeral.

jokes.text: John, clearly in distress, asks Moira to let 'your voice take five for a minute'
jokes.setup: Moira is calling out desperately to John who is having chest pains
jokes.punchline: John uses backstage theater language ('take five') to politely ask his wife to stop screaming at him while he has a medical episode
jokes.explanation: Very John Rose — understated, polite, enduring. The theater-world language ('take five') in a medical emergency is a nice specific detail. His gentle firmness with Moira even when he's in pain is both funny and characterful.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, I have other priorities. Like my skincare routine, my sleep schedule, and a very complicated relationship with carbs.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Okay, no one panic. Okay Stevie, get your car. John's a slow driver. We'll leave the programs behind. Or maybe we'll bring a stack for the trip?' Stevie: 'N-no, Mrs. Rose!'
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to organize a rush to the hospital
jokes.punchline: Even in a medical emergency, she considers bringing programs to fold in the car — and Stevie has to stop her
jokes.explanation: The callback to the programs in a genuine crisis is perfect Moira — she's panicking but her program obsession momentarily overrides rational thought. Stevie's horrified 'N-no, Mrs. Rose!' is a great straight-man reaction. The escalation of her momentary lapse is very funny.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't understand why we have to hike to have a picnic. Why can't we just have a picnic in the car? We could sit in the car, eat our food, and then drive away. That seems far more efficient.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Because that's not a picnic, David. That's just eating in a car.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, my specific romantic fantasy involves being fed grapes by several shirtless men while random horseback riders gallop past us in slow motion. So really, your idea of a picnic is just as unrealistic as mine.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Everybody move!' then immediately '- No! Not you, John!'
jokes.setup: Moira is rallying everyone to get moving in the emergency
jokes.punchline: She includes John in the command before realizing he's the one who needs to be still
jokes.explanation: Quick and efficient — Moira's command mode briefly overrides her care for John. The self-correction is perfectly timed. Great economy; the joke lives entirely in the beat between the two lines.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Do you want to turn around?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I can't make hiking decisions without my step-counting app.

jokes.text: Twyla is doing tarot card readings to raise money for a new dishwasher. She has learned tarot from one of her mother's ex-boyfriends who was 'a magician, and a gambling addict. But he was also really good at reading tarot cards. He predicted when he was going to leave my mom like, to the day.'
jokes.setup: Twyla explains where she learned to read tarot
jokes.punchline: The ex-boyfriend used tarot to predict his own abandonment of her mother — which she reports without any apparent distress
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and a perfect Twyla moment. The escalating dysfunction of her backstory (magician → gambling addict → tarot reader → predicted his own departure from her mother's life) is perfectly structured, and her cheerful, unbothered delivery of genuinely tragic information is the show's signature Twyla note. Highly original, character-specific, and dark in the most delightful way.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my god, are you okay? Did you break your leg? Are you bleeding? Do you need a tourniquet? I don't know how to make a tourniquet!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Should we call for help? Can you move? Oh god, we're going to die out here, aren't we?

jokes.text: 'Oh, one of my mom's ex-boyfriends was a magician, and a gambling addict. But he was also really good at reading tarot cards. He predicted when he was going to leave my mom like, to the day.'
jokes.setup: Patrick asks where Twyla learned to read tarot cards
jokes.punchline: Twyla's backstory involves a magician gambling addict who used tarot to predict his own departure — which is both darkly sad and completely on-brand for Twyla's chaotic upbringing, delivered with zero self-pity
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best Twyla joke and one of its overall highlights. Every detail escalates perfectly: magician → gambling addict → tarot card reader → predicted his own abandonment to the day. The final detail is the inspired twist — he used occult skills to plan his exit. Twyla's breezy delivery of this tragedy is what makes it land; the comedy comes entirely from her character. Exceptional character integration, high originality, very quotable. Strong 8+ moment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You are the most important thing in my world. Well, you and Caroline.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Who's Caroline?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: My 40-year-old wig.

jokes.text: Patrick dismisses the tarot reading: 'you can't actually see that in these cards.' Twyla: 'Well, I also need to interpret the cards.' Patrick: 'Okay, so maybe you could interpret something a little more positive?'
jokes.setup: Twyla has just predicted a boat capsizing and drowning for Alexis and Ted
jokes.punchline: Patrick's suggestion that Twyla could choose a more positive interpretation — treating tarot reading as a customer service issue
jokes.explanation: Patrick's pragmatic approach to supernatural doom — just ask for a different reading — is a nice character beat. The humor is in his reasonableness about something unreasonable.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm going to make some changes. Real changes. Starting tomorrow, I'm cutting back on everything—the shopping, the elaborate skin care regimen, the unnecessary purchases.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's great, Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, except for my pills. Those are non-negotiable. And it's really your fault for not being more successful, so I don't know what you want from me.

jokes.text: Twyla tries to give a positive reading: 'Oh, I also see a party. People dressed up all fancy in black.' Alexis: 'Like a black and white party?' Twyla: 'Mm, more like a funeral.'
jokes.setup: Twyla attempts to find something positive in the cards
jokes.punchline: Her 'positive' reading is people in black — which turns out to be a funeral, not a party
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. The three-beat structure is flawless: Twyla promises positivity → describes people in black → Alexis's hopeful guess (black and white party!) → 'more like a funeral.' The misdirection through Alexis's interpretation makes the punchline land harder. Twyla's gentle, unbothered correction is perfectly deadpan. Highly structured and economical.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Hospitals make me nervous. Last time I was in one, my cousin Derek died.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Different hospital, different cousin. Also named Derek.

jokes.text: David: 'Well, if you can track my eBay bid, then, by all means.' — responding to Patrick saying they don't need cell service, they have each other
jokes.setup: Patrick tells David to put his phone down on the hike — they don't need service
jokes.punchline: David reveals he's tracking an eBay bid — the specific thing he needs service for — which perfectly captures his priorities
jokes.explanation: eBay specifically is the right level of mundane specificity for David — not a luxury app, not something profound, just an eBay bid. His materialism expressed through online auction surveillance is good character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: We're going to need to get you prepped for surgery. Code Blue, stat.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Code Blue? I'm familiar with codes. I was in a production of Blue Bloods.

jokes.text: David complains the backpack is heavy. Patrick reveals: 'you had a pretty long list, and we needed ice packs to keep the cheese that you requested cold.' David: 'Well, did I get the ice pack one?'
jokes.setup: David complains about the weight of the backpacks
jokes.punchline: The weight is David's own fault — he requested cheese that required ice packs — and then he checks if he at least got the lighter bag
jokes.explanation: Good self-defeating logic — David is complaining about consequences of his own demands. His follow-up 'did I get the ice pack one?' shows he's still focused on his own comfort even while acknowledging the problem. Solid but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Which backpack has the first aid kit?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I packed the cheese preservation kit in this one.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, that's not a first aid kit.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I know, but cheese emergencies are more likely than medical ones.

jokes.text: David imagines 'a meadow somewhere, with some men on horseback, trotting in the background' as the ideal picnic setting
jokes.setup: David is complaining about the hike to the picnic spot
jokes.punchline: His ideal vision involves decorative horsemen — not participants, just background aesthetics
jokes.explanation: The men on horseback 'trotting in the background' as set dressing for his fantasy is quintessentially David — he's art-directing his own life. The specificity that they're not doing anything, just trotting decoratively, is the precise comic detail that elevates this.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, this is very thoughtful. You really went all out with the provisions here.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have to say though, there's not a single band-aid or antibiotic ointment in this entire bag. Which is great, because I'm sure nothing bad will happen.

jokes.text: David: 'Well, the app that's counting my steps isn't working, so I honestly don't know [whether to turn around].'
jokes.setup: Patrick asks if David wants to turn around
jokes.punchline: David's inability to make this decision without a step-counting app — the data-driven justification for giving up on exercise
jokes.explanation: David's logic is funny — he can't assess whether the hike counts as exercise without data confirmation. His outsourcing of bodily awareness to an app is a modern character note that fits him well.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: What are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh, nothing. Just browsing.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Did you know that the Titanic took two hours and forty minutes to sink?

jokes.text: David: 'I love that you've done this for us, and I think you look very dashing in your hiking clothes. It's just a long way to go for some cheese.'
jokes.setup: Patrick is frustrated with David's complaining; David tries to walk back his negativity
jokes.punchline: His apology is genuinely sweet but undone by the final clause — 'it's just a long way to go for some cheese' — which resurrects the very complaint he was apologizing for
jokes.explanation: David's inability to complete an apology without re-litigating his grievance is perfectly him. The sweetness of 'dashing in your hiking clothes' followed immediately by the cheese complaint is great rhythm. Shows how much he loves Patrick while also being completely incapable of suppressing his complaints.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I don't understand. Like, they're not missing. I know where they are.

jokes.text: A twig goes through David's shoe. His panicked 'Where's the bear?!' from Patrick followed by David's: 'No, I stepped on a branch. It went right through my shoe.'
jokes.setup: Patrick hears David cry out suddenly in the woods
jokes.punchline: Patrick's first thought is a bear — but David has just stepped on a stick. The bear fear is more reasonable than the reality, which is more absurd.
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy beat. Patrick's bear assumption is funnier than the actual accident, and the anticlimax of 'I stepped on a branch' is a nice deflation. The scene then extends with genuine care from Patrick, which grounds it.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I don't know, Ted. I just feel like... confused? But also like, not confused? It's like I'm feeling something but I don't know what the feeling is.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Alexis, that's called having emotions. Most people experience them on a regular basis.

jokes.text: Patrick didn't bring a first aid kit: 'It was either the first aid kit, or the ice packs.'
jokes.setup: David has a stick through his shoe and asks which bag has the first aid kit
jokes.punchline: Patrick chose the cheese ice packs over first aid supplies — a decision that now seems catastrophically wrong
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback. The cheese ice packs from earlier pay off here — David's demands are the direct cause of their inability to treat his injury. Economy is excellent: one line delivers the punchline and the callback simultaneously. The implied logic (cheese safety > human safety) is absurd and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm so worried about David being alone all the time.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not alone. I have Patrick.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, but like, before Patrick?

jokes.text: David uses hand sanitizer as a wound disinfectant: 'You think I'd come to a picnic without it?' then: 'We'll reapply later, but at least I don't have to worry about gangrene.'
jokes.setup: They have no first aid kit; David improvises
jokes.punchline: David's germophobia has accidentally made him prepared — his hand sanitizer becomes medical equipment, and he's pleased with himself about gangrene prevention
jokes.explanation: David's neuroses accidentally serving him well is a nice inversion. 'At least I don't have to worry about gangrene' is a very David way of finding optimism — through avoiding a catastrophic outcome rather than achieving a good one.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I can't handle the business operations of this motel on my own.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Because I'm not a businessperson. I'm a motel clerk who somehow became the manager, and now I'm expected to do everything from scheduling to finances to dealing with guests who think a motel is a five-star resort.

jokes.text: David's apology: 'I'm sorry for not appreciating you enough today. You've packed two big backpacks full of food, and not a Band-aid to be found in either of them, but still.'
jokes.setup: David attempts a sincere apology to Patrick for complaining
jokes.punchline: He can't help inserting a complaint about the missing Band-aids into his apology — making it both genuinely sweet and self-undermining
jokes.explanation: A really well-crafted character moment. The joke is structurally elegant — genuine apology undermined by a grievance parenthetical, rescued by 'but still.' It's a joke that reveals David's character (he can't help himself) while also being genuinely touching. The 'but still' is the perfect cap. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Your test results look good.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's wonderful news. You know, this reminds me of the time I was playing golf at Pebble Beach...

jokes.text: After the emotional moment of David saying they should finish the hike, he immediately adds: 'Also, I'm starving, and I need to eat something.'
jokes.setup: Patrick and David have a touching moment about continuing the hike together
jokes.punchline: David immediately follows the sincere sentiment with a practical food complaint, undercutting the romance without negating it
jokes.explanation: The timing is everything — a beat of sincerity followed by David's most basic concern. It's funny and endearing simultaneously because it confirms that David being hungry is as real and urgent to him as the emotional moment. The brevity makes it land.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's wonderful news about Johnny, but I'm afraid I have some concerns about my own health. I've been experiencing a mysterious tingling in my left eyelid, and I'm fairly certain it's either a rare neurological condition or a sign that I'm aging.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: That's just a muscle twitch, Mrs. Rose.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A twitch? How pedestrian. I was rather hoping for something more exotic. Perhaps something that would require a specialist in Switzerland?

jokes.text: Patrick: 'I should probably get out in front, David. You have no idea where you're going.' David: 'Okay. That's fair.' Patrick: 'Ow!'
jokes.setup: Patrick asserts leadership of the hike right after their sweet moment
jokes.punchline: He immediately injures himself taking the lead, undercutting his authority
jokes.explanation: Beautifully efficient physical comedy — three lines and a sound effect. The callback to David's earlier injury is implicit. Patrick's 'Ow!' immediately after asserting competence is a great anti-climax. Perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm fine. I'm just worried about my eyelash extensions. I haven't been able to get them done in like three weeks.

jokes.text: Alexis is doing an image search for 'intense boating disasters' to see 'what I would look like after a catamaran accident'
jokes.setup: Ted catches Alexis on her phone looking at something disturbing
jokes.punchline: She's pre-visualizing disaster as an aesthetic — not checking survival rates, but imagining her look post-accident
jokes.explanation: Only Alexis would respond to a death prediction by researching her potential appearance post-disaster. It's absurd, vain, and perfectly character-specific. The specificity of 'what I would look like' rather than 'how likely is this' is the whole joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We stopped hiking because Patrick got injured, and if Moira found out, she would literally kill me. And I don't mean that figuratively — she would actually end my life.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'What if something happens to David? I'm basically his only friend. Or my Dad. David and my mom sometimes gang up on him, and I'm basically his only friend.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is confessing her fear of leaving her family while she goes to the Galapagos
jokes.punchline: She describes herself as David's 'only friend' twice — once for David, once for her Dad — revealing both her genuine love for them and her blissfully inflated sense of her own role
jokes.explanation: The double use of 'I'm basically his only friend' — applied to both David and her Dad — is the joke. She's being genuinely caring but the framing also reveals her self-centered worldview. The observation about David and Moira ganging up on John is also accurate and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, I know we've had our ups and downs, but I can't imagine my life without you. Will you marry me?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, of course. But first, how many carats?

jokes.text: Ted's reassurance about hating the trip: 'if we get there, and we hate it, well, we'll probably have to wait it out a bit, just because it's pretty far away, and it would be pretty tough to re-book our travel arrangements'
jokes.setup: Ted is trying to comfort Alexis by saying they can always leave if they hate it
jokes.punchline: His reassurance immediately qualifies itself into meaninglessness — they're essentially trapped there
jokes.explanation: Ted's cheerful honesty about the limitations of his comfort undermines the comfort. The cascade of qualifications is funny because he's aware of what he's doing but presses forward anyway. Good but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Can I get another reading?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Sure. Oh wow, that's interesting.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: You're going to die.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Yeah, multiple times actually.

jokes.text: Roland in the hospital waiting room: 'Well, you know, uh... I'm just gonna go ahead and say it. It's been a long time, that's usually not a good sign.'
jokes.setup: Everyone is anxiously waiting for news about John's condition
jokes.punchline: Roland voices the exact thought everyone is suppressing — and presents it as an act of bravery ('I'm just gonna go ahead and say it')
jokes.explanation: Classic Roland — his social filter is absent, so he says what no one wants said. The framing of 'I'm just gonna go ahead and say it' as if he's doing the room a favor is perfectly obtuse. Stevie's correction ('I don't think Mrs. Rose needs to hear that') is the straight-man response.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Oh, a golden ring! That's a sign of prosperity and good fortune coming your way.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Wait... is that a beer stain?

jokes.text: Moira: 'In the Balkan Peninsula they say, "silence is but an empty serving platter." I'm sure there's a deluge of benign reasons they're keeping John back there.'
jokes.setup: Stevie has told Roland that Moira doesn't need to hear his pessimism
jokes.punchline: Moira offers a fake Balkan proverb to justify talking more, then immediately pivots to anxious optimism
jokes.explanation: Another Moira fake-international wisdom moment, this time deployed to justify her own need to fill silence. 'Silence is but an empty serving platter' is a genuinely well-crafted fake aphorism. The contrast between the grand Balkan framing and her anxious reassurance is funny. Callbacks to the episode's opening Bosnian bit.

jokes.text: Roland: 'He probably just roped them into one of his longwinded stories.' immediately followed by: 'Or he could be on a table with a triple bypass.'
jokes.setup: The group is trying to be optimistic about why John's taking so long
jokes.punchline: Roland offers a benign explanation (longwinded story) then immediately escalates to 'triple bypass' in the same breath
jokes.explanation: Roland's oblivious escalation from 'longwinded story' to 'triple bypass' is a great joke. The lack of a beat between them — as if these are equivalent possibilities — is where the comedy lives. His inability to self-regulate in a crisis is perfect character work.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Mr. Rose is gonna be fine, he has to be. I mean, I can't be left alone with this motel. I don't do math.'
jokes.setup: The group is waiting for news about John; Stevie tries to be reassuring
jokes.punchline: Her reason for needing John to survive is entirely selfish and professional — she can't run the motel because she doesn't do math
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The emotional setup — 'he has to be fine' — primes the audience for a sincere reason, making the pivot to 'I can't be left alone with this motel. I don't do math.' even more deflating and hilarious. It's perfectly Stevie: dry, self-aware, unexpectedly revealing her own limitations, and secretly showing she cares about John by framing his survival in purely practical terms. Extremely quotable. Could only come from this character.

jokes.text: Doctor reveals John has severe heartburn, gave him an antacid, and then adds: 'And I drained a 20-footer, and finished with an eagle.'
jokes.setup: The doctor arrives with John's test results — the family is braced for devastating news
jokes.punchline: After delivering the benign medical verdict, the doctor casually adds a golf score — revealing he took time off to play during John's tests
jokes.explanation: The tonal whiplash from medical update to golf score is the joke. The doctor's casual addition of his eagle reveals what he was doing during the 'tests,' and connects to John's earlier Pebble Beach story mention. Good misdirection and surprise.

jokes.text: The doctor mentions 'there have been some fatal cases... of heartburn.' John immediately: 'Did I?'
jokes.setup: Moira pushes back on the heartburn diagnosis, suggesting it could be more serious; the doctor acquiesces and mentions fatal heartburn cases
jokes.punchline: John, who presumably did NOT say this, immediately distances himself from the statement — he's been with Moira long enough to know when she's putting words in mouths
jokes.explanation: Extraordinarily economical — two words ('Did I?') that reveal John's entire relationship with Moira. He knows the doctor has been pressured into saying something he didn't originate. His mild, weary correction is perfectly timed and deeply character-specific. The joke also retroactively reframes Moira's manipulation as visible to everyone including John.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Yep, I'm just really not looking forward to folding those programs.' — her response when asked if everything is okay after John's safe return
jokes.setup: Everyone is emotionally relieved; Stevie is asked if she's okay
jokes.punchline: Her 'not looking forward to' is the programs — the entire emotional roller-coaster of the episode distilled into dread of a clerical task
jokes.explanation: A perfect Stevie beat. After a genuine emotional scare, she expresses her 'okay-ness' through the most mundane possible concern. The callback to the programs also closes a structural loop in the episode. Her understatement in emotional moments is one of her defining characteristics.

jokes.text: John: 'Thank you, Doctor. I'll have to call you with the rest of that Pebble Beach story.' Moira: 'Oh John, the man's done enough!'
jokes.setup: John is thanking the doctor and wants to continue a golf story he apparently started earlier
jokes.punchline: Moira's exasperated 'the man's done enough!' — meant as gratitude but also gently chiding John's obliviousness to the doctor's time
jokes.explanation: A gentle button on the hospital scene. John's social obliviousness in trying to resume a story after a medical scare is very him, and Moira's response is simultaneously grateful and exasperated. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: David carrying Patrick up the mountain on his back after the branch-through-shoe injury
jokes.setup: Patrick has injured his foot on a branch; they need to continue the hike
jokes.punchline: David — who spent the entire episode complaining about the hike — is now literally carrying Patrick up the mountain
jokes.explanation: Visually strong and emotionally resonant. The comedic irony of David — the episode's most vocal hike-complainer — being the one who physically carries his partner up the mountain is a beautiful payoff to everything that came before. It's funny and sweet simultaneously. High character integration: it shows David's love through the very activity he resisted. Strong rewatch bonus.

jokes.text: Patrick to the injured David: 'I wouldn't have made you hike all this way if I didn't think it was gonna be worth it. I know you a little better than that.'
jokes.setup: David is taking in the view after carrying Patrick up the mountain
jokes.punchline: Patrick saying he 'knows David' — while David literally just carried him up a mountain on his back, which Patrick perhaps underestimated David would do
jokes.explanation: More sweet than funny — serves the romantic arc more than the comedy. The irony that Patrick 'knows' David but didn't predict the level of suffering David would put himself through is mild.

jokes.text: David: 'Okay, I didn't carry you up a mountain not to eat cheese after, so...' insisting on the picnic despite the proposal context
jokes.setup: Patrick suggests maybe skipping the picnic to take in the view
jokes.punchline: David's insistence on the cheese — having hauled it up a mountain at great personal cost — is non-negotiable, even as Patrick is clearly about to propose
jokes.explanation: The cheese has become a running commitment — David needs to vindicate his suffering. The comedy of him demanding cheese picnic logistics while Patrick is building to a marriage proposal is perfectly timed. 'I didn't carry you up a mountain not to eat cheese after' is a very quotable and David-specific line.

jokes.text: After Patrick proposes and David accepts emotionally, David's first words are: 'Are these 24-karat?' — about the ring
jokes.setup: Patrick has just proposed; David has emotionally said yes
jokes.punchline: His first verbal response is to assess the ring's gold content — materialism breaking through the emotion
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode. Perfect structure: the most romantic moment of the episode (a proposal scene built over 20 minutes) is punctuated by David's most David instinct — appraising the jewelry. 'Are these 24-karat?' followed immediately by 'It's a yes, it's a yes! I love you.' is flawless. The rapid pivot to 'yes' shows the question was never actually in doubt; the karat-check is pure reflex. Couldn't come from any other character on any other show. Extremely quotable. A genuine standout moment.

jokes.text: Alexis goes back to the café under pretense of 'popping in to see how you're doing, girl' — immediately caught by Twyla: 'Is this about the card reading earlier?'
jokes.setup: Alexis clearly wants another tarot reading to replace the bad one
jokes.punchline: Twyla sees through her immediately; Alexis's denial ('What? No.') and then immediate confession is the joke
jokes.explanation: Functional character beat — Alexis's superstition overrides her pride, and Twyla's perceptiveness (often underestimated) is a nice note. Competent but not a standout joke.

jokes.text: Twyla warns: 'I should warn you, I think the deck may be cursed. I predicted four other drownings today.'
jokes.setup: Alexis asks for another reading; Twyla agrees but warns her
jokes.punchline: The deck is cursed — evidenced by four drowning predictions in one day — delivered with complete matter-of-factness
jokes.explanation: Twyla's deadpan reporting of four drowning predictions as a diagnostic symptom of deck-curse is very funny. Her casual approach to mass-predicting death is consistent with her character's signature dark cheerfulness.

jokes.text: Twyla describes the Ten of Cups as showing Alexis's family with 'a big golden ring of light around them. Like you all had something to celebrate.' — then adds: 'Either that or it's a stain from a beer bottle. Because my mom's ex gave me the cards.' Alexis: 'Ew.'
jokes.setup: Twyla delivers a beautiful, meaningful reading about Alexis's family celebrating something
jokes.punchline: She immediately undercuts it by suggesting the 'golden ring' might just be a beer-bottle stain from her alcoholic ex-boyfriend's tarot deck
jokes.explanation: An excellent episode-closing joke. The structure is beautiful: a genuinely touching moment (Alexis's family celebrating — which we know is the engagement) is simultaneously honored and undercut. The beer stain callback to the ex-boyfriend's deck is perfect Twyla — she doesn't protect the meaningful interpretation, she just honestly reports the ambiguity. 'Ew' as Alexis's final word is the right button. The joke works on multiple levels: funny, character-true, and the audience knows the 'golden ring' is the engagement ring, which adds resonance.

jokes.text: 'Either that or it's a stain from a beer bottle. Because my mom's ex gave me the cards.' / 'Ew.'
jokes.setup: Twyla interprets the 'golden ring of light' in Alexis's reading as prosperity
jokes.punchline: She offers an alternative explanation: it might be a beer bottle stain on the card from the gambling addict magician — the spiritual and the squalid in one sentence
jokes.explanation: A brilliant ending joke and perfect callback to the magician ex. The juxtaposition of 'golden ring of light = prosperity' versus 'beer bottle stain from a gambling addict' is a wonderful deflation of the episode's emotional optimism — but it's done with such warmth it still works as a hopeful ending. Alexis's 'Ew.' is the perfect one-word button. Exceptional character integration, high originality, and great economy. One of the episode's top jokes.

tmdb_overview: Patrick tries to take David on a romantic hike, while Johnny has a health scare that sends everyone into a panic.
insight: Character comedy drives a slower, steadier episode as the Rose family tackles the outdoors.
